title: AI generation streaming
summary: The AI Client, which runs AI tools inside WordPress, is gaining the ability to stream responses as they are generated rather than waiting for a complete answer. This means when you use an AI feature in WordPress, you'll see text appear progressively (like ChatGPT does) instead of a long pause followed by the full response. For plugin developers, this unlocks richer AI experiences, and for users it makes AI tools feel faster and more responsive during longer tasks.

title: AI embeddings for content search
summary: Embeddings turn your content into numerical representations that capture meaning, enabling searches that understand what you're asking for rather than just matching keywords. Instead of searching for exact phrases, you could ask 'how to improve site speed' and find relevant posts even if they use different wording. This feature lays the groundwork for smarter, meaning-based search across your WordPress site, though it's aimed primarily at plugin developers this cycle.

title: Connectors authentication methods
summary: Connectors, which manage how WordPress connects to external services like AI providers, are expanding beyond API keys to support username and application password authentication. The system is also moving toward declarative connection forms where you can fill out URLs, select default models, and configure credentials in a consistent way. This makes it simpler and more flexible for site administrators to connect WordPress to third-party tools without diving into code.

title: Guidelines for editorial rules
summary: Guidelines is a new feature that lets you define writing rules, brand voice, and editorial standards directly in WordPress. You can create guidelines (like 'use active voice' or 'avoid jargon'), import and export them between sites, and have AI tools respect them automatically. This brings a structured, persistent way to keep your content consistent when collaborating with teams or AI assistants, so your site's voice stays on-brand no matter who is writing.

title: Organized command palette with recents
summary: The command palette (the quick-search tool you open with keyboard shortcuts) now groups commands into clear sections: recently used, suggested, and matching results. Your recently used commands are saved to your profile so they persist across sessions, and the visual design is cleaner and easier to scan. This makes it faster to jump to the actions you use most often, turning the command palette into a personalized shortcut hub.

title: Admin color scheme in Site Editor
summary: The Site Editor sidebar and shell now respect your chosen WordPress admin color scheme instead of always displaying a dark background. If you've customized your admin colors in Settings, those preferences now carry through consistently to the Site Editor. This ensures a unified look across all editing interfaces and honors your personal color preferences everywhere you work in WordPress.

title: DataViews and DataForms improvements
summary: DataViews (the list-based interface for managing posts, pages, templates, and patterns) is being migrated to the new Design System for a more polished, consistent look. Quick Edit and the editor sidebar inspector are being unified so editing metadata feels the same no matter where you do it. The Site Editor's list screens are also becoming more extensible with new REST endpoints, letting plugin developers register custom views and form configurations for their own data.

title: Dedicated Identity section
summary: A new Design → Identity screen gathers your site's core identity settings—logo, favicon, site title, and tagline—into one convenient place. The screen includes an inline media editor so you can crop and adjust your logo and favicon without leaving the page. This makes foundational branding settings easy to find and update without hunting through templates or digging into the Settings menu.

title: Design System component library maturation
summary: The shared component library and theming system that power the editor interface are graduating ThemeProvider from experimental to stable. Public token names for colors (background, foreground, stroke) are being finalized, and new customization tokens for corner radius and element sizing are being added. Across the editor, more menus (transforms, style variations, block options) are adopting the improved Design System components for a more cohesive, modern interface.

title: On This Day dashboard widget
summary: A new dashboard widget called 'On This Day' resurfaces content you published on this date in past years, similar to memory features on social platforms. It's designed to inspire you by looking back at what you've written before and motivate you to create new content today that will resurface as future reminders. This brings a touch of nostalgia and engagement directly to your WordPress dashboard.

title: Persistent admin bar across editors
summary: The familiar WordPress admin bar (the black toolbar across the top when you're logged in) is now accessible in the Site Editor and Block Editor, no longer limited to the traditional admin screens. The design has been refreshed: it drops the 'Howdy' greeting, swaps the home icon for your site icon, rounds the profile avatar, and replaces old Dashicons with modern SVG icons. This makes navigation consistent and familiar no matter where you are editing in WordPress.

title: Revisions interface enhancements
summary: After introducing visual revisions in 7.0, this release focuses on making them easier to read and navigate. Planned improvements include a sparkline chart in the scrubbing toolbar that visualizes the frequency and timing of changes, and persistent URLs so you can share a direct link to a specific revision with collaborators. These refinements make it faster to understand and browse the history of changes to your content.

title: Abilities API expansion
summary: The Abilities API provides a structured, queryable way to describe what a WordPress site can do, helping developers and AI tools understand available features and capabilities. This cycle adds improved querying and filtering, and implements a set of core abilities like managing site settings, accessing current user information, and general site awareness. This makes it easier for tools (both human-built and AI-driven) to discover and interact with WordPress functionality programmatically.

title: Block Bindings for list and inner blocks
summary: Block Bindings, which connect block content to dynamic data sources, now supports binding list-item blocks and inner blocks. This means more parts of your page layout—not just individual blocks—can pull content dynamically from custom fields, post metadata, or other sources. For editors, it opens up richer, data-driven page designs without custom code.

title: Enforced iframed editor for block themes
summary: The post editor now runs inside an isolated frame (an iframe) that keeps the editing canvas separate from the admin's styles. This allows viewport-relative units and media queries to work correctly against the canvas, not the browser window, so what you see while editing matches what visitors see on the front end. In 7.1, iframe mode becomes enforced for block-based themes; classic themes will follow in a future release. Blocks need to be updated to Block API version 3 to work in the iframed editor, and a migration guide is available to help developers.

title: Extended Unicode support in email addresses
summary: WordPress is expanding support for Unicode characters in email addresses so non-ASCII characters (like accented letters or non-Latin scripts) can be stored and validated correctly. Core functions like is_email(), sanitize_email(), and antispambot() are being updated to handle international email addresses. This helps WordPress better reflect its global, multilingual audience and ensures people can use email addresses in their native languages.

title: React 19 upgrade
summary: WordPress is upgrading the React library that powers the block editor from version 18 to version 19. This update brings new APIs, improved TypeScript types, and changed behaviors. It will first land in the Gutenberg plugin as an opt-in experiment before eventually merging into Core. Plugin and theme developers are encouraged to test their code early by enabling the 'React 19' experiment in Gutenberg to catch compatibility issues before the upgrade reaches Core.

title: Icon API expansion
summary: After 7.0 introduced the Icon block and a basic icon registry, 7.1 opens the Icon API to third parties. Plugin and theme developers can now register and unregister their own icons using new public functions, organize icons into collections (like the Font Library), and ship branded icon sets. The Icon block itself gains flip and rotate controls, and blocks like Navigation, Breadcrumbs, and Details will let you pick icons from the registry. Alongside the API, the core icon set is getting a visual refresh with stroke-based designs for a more modern, consistent look.

title: Classic block deprecation
summary: The Classic block, which wraps the old TinyMCE editor, is being deprecated and removed from the block inserter in 7.1. Existing Classic blocks in your content will continue to work, and you can still enable the block if needed, but it won't appear when you search for new blocks to add. This is the first step toward making the Classic block opt-in, so sites that don't rely on it get a lighter, faster editor. Improved migration and conversion tools are being developed to help move content to modern blocks.

title: Playlist block
summary: A new Playlist block lets you display a list of audio files with waveform visualizations. Visitors can browse and play tracks directly on the page, and the waveform gives a visual sense of the audio's structure. This is especially useful for podcasters, musicians, or anyone publishing multiple audio files who wants an interactive, visually engaging way to present them.

title: Table of Contents block
summary: The new Table of Contents block automatically generates a navigable list of links to the headings in your content. Visitors can click a heading in the table of contents and jump directly to that section, making long posts and documentation easier to navigate. The block updates automatically when you add or remove headings, so your table of contents always stays in sync with your content.

title: Tabs block
summary: The new Tabs block organizes content into tabbed panels that visitors can click to switch between. Each tab holds its own content, letting you present multiple pieces of information in a compact, space-saving layout. This is useful for things like FAQs, product features, or any scenario where you want to let readers choose what to view without scrolling through everything.

title: Gallery block lightbox refinements
summary: The Gallery block's lightbox view (full-screen image viewing) is getting mobile-friendly refinements including swipe gesture indicators and opt-in captions. These improvements make browsing image galleries on phones and tablets smoother and more intuitive, with clearer visual cues about how to navigate and the option to show descriptive captions in the lightbox overlay.

title: HTML block with editable nested blocks
summary: The HTML block is gaining new block support that allows editable blocks to exist inside custom HTML. This is especially valuable when working with AI-generated sites, since large language models often produce custom HTML markup. Instead of having to edit raw code, you'll be able to interact with nested blocks inside that HTML in the normal visual editor, making AI-generated layouts more maintainable.

title: Mark as decorative for images
summary: The Image block now includes a 'Mark as decorative' toggle that hides decorative images from screen readers. When enabled, assistive technologies skip the image, improving the experience for blind and low-vision users by removing clutter from the page's semantic structure. This helps you follow accessibility best practices by distinguishing between meaningful images that need descriptions and purely decorative ones that don't.

title: Embed shortcode to block transform
summary: The Embed block now recognizes the legacy [embed] shortcode and automatically converts it into a proper Embed block when you paste or convert shortcodes. Instead of seeing raw shortcode text in your content, you get the full visual embed with all the block's formatting and preview options. This makes migrating older content smoother and keeps your editing experience consistent.

title: Shortcode block smart transforms
summary: The Shortcode block can now automatically detect when pasted text matches a registered shortcode and offer to transform it into the appropriate block. For example, pasting a gallery shortcode might prompt you to convert it into a Gallery block. This reduces manual conversion work and helps you take advantage of modern block features instead of relying on legacy shortcodes.

title: Group block background gradients
summary: The Group block now supports background gradients through a new background.gradient setting, allowing gradients and background images to coexist without conflicts. You can layer a gradient over an image or use a gradient alone for colorful, modern designs. While currently limited to the Group block, this feature is designed to expand to more blocks, and developers can enable it for custom blocks using a simple filter.

title: Writing flow and drag-and-drop improvements
summary: A dedicated effort is focused on smoothing out everyday writing and content-arranging tasks. This includes improving how drag-and-drop behaves when moving blocks around, ensuring multi-selection works reliably on touch devices, and removing small friction points in the writing experience. The goal is to make creating and organizing content feel more fluid and intuitive, especially on tablets and mobile devices.

title: Enhanced Notes with suggestions and reactions
summary: The Notes feature (inline comments and feedback within the editor) is gaining a rich set of improvements: you can leave notes on specific content within a block or across multiple blocks, add rich text formatting to notes, receive notifications when someone replies or follows your note, react with emojis, view a minified notes panel, and apply suggestions directly. These features turn Notes into a powerful collaboration tool for asynchronous feedback, making it easier to review and refine content with teammates or clients without leaving the editor.

title: Real-time collaboration progress
summary: Real-time collaboration aims to let multiple people (and AI assistants) edit content simultaneously without post locks or conflicts. Major strategic decisions remain around whether to ship the full feature or just the underlying architecture in 7.1, and which storage mechanism to use. A dedicated outreach and testing effort is underway to ensure the feature is reliable and ready. If successful, real-time collaboration will fundamentally change how teams work together in WordPress, making co-editing as seamless as Google Docs.

title: Display inherited styles
summary: When styling a block, it's often unclear whether styles come from the theme, a parent block, or global settings. This feature surfaces inherited styles directly in the sidebar, showing you the source of each style property. You'll be able to see at a glance what's being applied globally versus locally, making it easier to decide whether to override a style for one block or edit the global setting to affect all blocks.

title: Interactive states styling
summary: You can now style how blocks respond to user interaction—hover, focus, and active states—directly in the editor without writing CSS. For example, you can make a button change color when someone hovers over it, or style a link's focus state for better keyboard navigation. The feature works for both Global Styles (applying to all blocks of a type) and individual block instances. Future work will extend this to custom states like styling the current menu item.

title: Pattern editing iterations
summary: After WordPress 7.0 shifted pattern editing to focus on content changes rather than exposing every tool for every block, this cycle refines that experience. Work focuses on user experience improvements based on feedback, bug fixes, and general maintenance to make editing patterns smoother and more intuitive. The goal is to balance simplicity (treating a pattern as a single unit) with the power users need to customize content within patterns.

title: Responsive styling for blocks
summary: Responsive styling lets you define how a block looks at different screen sizes—like setting a larger font on desktop and a smaller one on mobile—directly in the editor without custom CSS. The feature uses the same style states system that powers interactive states, and works for both Global Styles and individual block instances. This makes responsive design a built-in, first-class part of the editing experience, so your site looks great on every device without diving into code.

title: Viewport breakpoint customization
summary: Themes can now define custom breakpoints (the screen widths where responsive styles kick in) in theme.json. This gives theme authors control over when layouts shift from desktop to tablet to mobile, and provides more flexible, customizable responsive styling across the site. Combined with the responsive styling features, it ensures your theme's breakpoints align with your design needs.

title: Client-side media processing
summary: WordPress is gaining robust client-side media handling that processes images and videos in your browser before uploading. Features include HEIC and Ultra HDR image support, GIF-to-video conversion, resilient uploads that retry on failure and resume after a crash or going offline, video transcoding to web-safe formats, optimization of previously uploaded media, and automatic poster image generation for videos. This makes uploads faster, more reliable, and more capable without server-side dependencies.

title: Media editor modal
summary: The new Media editor modal replaces the inline cropping tool in the Block Editor with a dedicated, full-featured editing experience. You still click the familiar Crop button to start, but the modal now offers freeform and aspect-ratio cropping, flip and rotation (with fine-grained control and snap-to-angle), and metadata editing all in one place. This brings a more powerful and polished image-editing workflow directly into WordPress.

title: Media gallery improvements
summary: Galleries are becoming more dynamic and easier to build. Improvements include better handling when converting the legacy gallery shortcode, dynamic galleries that can automatically pull or sort media attached to the current post, and a quicker path in the block inserter's media tab to see attached images with thumbnails shown directly. These changes make assembling and managing image galleries faster and more flexible.

title: Speculative loading performance update
summary: When WordPress detects that your site has both object caching and page caching enabled, it will now prefetch and prerender pages more aggressively (moving from 'conservative' to 'moderate' eagerness). This makes navigation feel faster for visitors on well-equipped sites, as the browser anticipates and loads the next page before you click. The change is automatic and adapts to your server's capabilities.

title: View Transitions plugin development
summary: The View Transitions feature plugin is in active development to bring smooth, animated transitions between pages on the front end of your site, similar to single-page app experiences. When a visitor navigates from one page to another, elements can fade, slide, or morph instead of the page abruptly reloading. The plugin is available for testing today, and contributors are welcome to help move it toward eventual Core inclusion.

title: Enhanced Responsive Images plugin development
summary: The Enhanced Responsive Images plugin is being developed to compute more accurate 'sizes' attribute values in block themes, so browsers download appropriately sized images for each visitor's screen. This reduces bandwidth waste and speeds up page loads by ensuring mobile users don't download huge desktop images. The plugin is available for testing, and contributors are encouraged to help refine it before potential Core integration.
